repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

body:
## Problem

All wasm parsers shared a single global TSWasmStore via ts_parser_set_wasm_store(). A TSWasmStore is owned by exactly one parser: ts_parser_delete() frees it through ts_wasm_store_delete(). So the first wasm parser collected freed the shared store, leaving the global pointer dangling, and creating the next wasm parser dereferenced freed memory in ts_wasm_store_reset() -> wasmtime_store_context(), crashing with SIGSEGV.

```
Exception Type:    EXC_BAD_ACCESS (SIGSEGV)
Exception Subtype: KERN_INVALID_ADDRESS at 0x00000000000573ac
Exception Codes:   0x0000000000000001, 0x00000000000573ac

Termination Reason:  Namespace SIGNAL, Code 11, Segmentation fault: 11
Terminating Process: exc handler [71000]


VM Region Info: 0x573ac is not in any region.  Bytes before following region: 4301753428
      REGION TYPE                    START - END         [ VSIZE] PRT/MAX SHRMOD  REGION DETAIL
      UNUSED SPACE AT START
--->  
      __TEXT                      1006d0000-1017d8000    [ 17.0M] r-x/r-x SM=COW  /opt/local/bin/nvim

Thread 0 Crashed::  Dispatch queue: com.apple.main-thread
0   nvim                          	       0x100d7a04c wasmtime_store_context + 0
1   nvim                          	       0x1006f5f1c ts_wasm_store_reset + 52
2   nvim                          	       0x1006da920 ts_parser_reset + 80
3   nvim                          	       0x1006da858 ts_parser_set_language + 24
4   nvim                          	       0x1013e8d9c tslua_push_parser + 308
5   libluajit-5.1.2.1.1774896198.dylib	       0x10206afd4 lj_BC_FUNCC + 44
6   libluajit-5.1.2.1.1774896198.dylib	       0x102077df4 lua_pcall + 156
7   nvim                          	       0x1013de4d4 nlua_pcall + 132
8   nvim                          	       0x1013e4c74 nlua_schedule_event + 160
9   nvim                          	       0x10157b90c state_handle_k_event + 340
10  nvim                          	       0x10129a97c insert_handle_key + 300
11  nvim                          	       0x10157b628 state_enter + 136
12  nvim                          	       0x101294e74 insert_enter + 2764
13  nvim                          	       0x101292a4c edit + 396
14  nvim                          	       0x10146bd40 nv_edit + 2124
15  nvim                          	       0x101466db0 normal_execute + 9952
16  nvim                          	       0x10157b628 state_enter + 136
17  nvim                          	       0x1013f305c main + 16688
18  dyld                          	       0x186833e00 start + 6992
```

## Solution

Give each parser its own store via ts_wasm_store_new() instead. This is the 1:1 store-per-parser model intended by tree-sitter (see tree-sitter/tree-sitter#3454): the global store remains only as the language loader, ts_parser_delete() cleanly frees each parser's own store, and the wasm engine is shared safely because ts_wasm_store_new() clones its engine reference internally.

AI-assisted: Claude Code

comment_regular_data.body: Note that this codepath is not exercised by tests (which is why it could break so badly). If we want to take this seriously, we should add a workflow that builds with WASM and tests actually loading parsers. (Suitable WASM fixtures can be downloaded from GH releases.)

Before this patch, nvim 0.12.2 happily loads wasm modules, but crashes with the mentioned segfault. After this patch, it crashes with another segfault fixed by https://github.com/tree-sitter/tree-sitter/pull/5605. After upgrading to tree-sitter 0.26.9 and this patch, nvim appears to be perfectly functional with wasm tree-sitter modules (if ENABLE_WASMTIME is enabled).

> Before this patch, nvim 0.12.2 happily loads wasm modules, but crashes with the mentioned segfault.

Yes, I know, hence my comment about this needing test coverage.

> After this patch, it crashes with another segfault fixed by https://github.com/tree-sitter/tree-sitter/pull/5605.

Are you testing against `master`? That should pull in a tree-sitter prerelease with the fix (but I'll double-check and bump if not).

**EDIT:** https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/40165

> Are you testing against master? That should pull in a tree-sitter prerelease with the fix (but I'll double-check and bump if not).

I tested against 0.12.2, but the pre-release is [fb348c3](https://github.com/tree-sitter/tree-sitter/commit/fb348c34939dd3cd5b0a67839b11e316d3333376) which from Apr 27 and the issue was fixed in May.

Not sure we'll backport this. As I wrote, WASM support currently is not in a state where we can officially maintain this; it remains best-effort bleeding-edge. (I'll probably also need to bump the wasmtime version to match.)

> Not sure we'll backport this. As I wrote, WASM support currently is not in a state where we can officially maintain this; it remains best-effort bleeding-edge. (I'll probably also need to bump the wasmtime version to match.)

That's fair. That said, it does work. My build is using wasmtime v36.0.6, tree-sitter v0.26.9, and nvim v0.12.2 with this patch.
(You can rebase(!) on latest master now to pull in the tree-sitter fix.)

Botched dep bump, but otherwise it seems work work!

```patch
diff --git a/src/nvim/CMakeLists.txt b/src/nvim/CMakeLists.txt
index 856f92e738..8f923a2add 100644
--- a/src/nvim/CMakeLists.txt
+++ b/src/nvim/CMakeLists.txt
@@ -53,7 +53,7 @@
 endif()

 if(ENABLE_WASMTIME)
-  find_package(Wasmtime 36.0.6 EXACT REQUIRED)
+  find_package(Wasmtime 36.0.9 EXACT REQUIRED)
   target_link_libraries(main_lib INTERFACE wasmtime)
   target_compile_definitions(nvim_bin PRIVATE HAVE_WASMTIME)
 endif()
 ```
See what I mean by "needs test coverage"?
The only requested suggestion has been made.
Well, technically the main requested suggestion was to add a test...

I see. Sorry, I interpreted this as more of a note to the maintainers. This will be my first contribution to neovim, so "adding a new target to the test infrastructure" is... idk about 10x more involved than fixing a single segfault.

Will I get support (from you and/or other maintainers) for adding that new test target and test case? Note that this is starting to get https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/39008 involved, because this segfault occurs in wasmtime.

> Note that this codepath is not exercised by tests (which is why it could break so badly). If we want to take this seriously, we should add a workflow that builds with WASM and tests actually loading parsers. (Suitable WASM fixtures can be downloaded from GH releases.)

Do you actually know where any suitable prebuilt wasm modules happen to be located? I just built my own locally.

> I see. Sorry, I interpreted this as more of a note to the maintainers. This will be my first contribution to neovim, so "adding a new target to the test infrastructure" is... idk about 10x more involved than fixing a single segfault.

You don't have the "First contributor" badge of ~shame~ pride anymore ;)

But you're right, this is part of a larger discussion on how seriously we want to take WASM parser support. It need not necessarily be part of _this_ PR, but should then be a direct followup -- otherwise we run in a circle of "works now" to "broke again somewhere" to "I'm just fixing my use case", which is not sustainable.

And if you are interested in contributing more (which would be very welcome!), you'll need to touch the tests sooner or later ;) (And yes, there will be help, within the constraints of available time.)

You also don't have to start from scratch: We used to at least have a build target (but no test) for WASM support; it was removed in https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/35656 because the feature itself was broken so the test was useless and just cost time.

Regarding WASM parsers: They are part of the release artifacts of most maintained<sup>1</sup> parsers, e.g., https://github.com/tree-sitter-grammars/tree-sitter-query/releases/tag/v0.8.0. (If you use one for a bundled parser you'll need to make sure it's used over the native build, but that would allow you to verify that the results match, not just that it doesn't segfault.)

---

<sup>1</sup> No True Scotsman Fallacy
given that this is already guarded by `HAVE_WASMTIME`, I don't see why we need to block it.

title: fix(treesitter): give each wasm parser its own TSWasmStore
